Categories, functors, and the Yoneda lemma

A disciplined way to say "arrows between things behave like the things themselves." From objects and morphisms to the universal property of the pullback and the adjunction that constructs free groups.

blocks.html:
1. What is a category?

A category $\mathcal{C}$ consists of:

  • a class of objects $\operatorname{ob}\mathcal{C}$;
  • for every ordered pair $(A,B)$, a set $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(A,B)$ of morphisms (or arrows) $f\colon A\to B$;
  • a composition law $\operatorname{Hom}(B,C)\times\operatorname{Hom}(A,B)\to\operatorname{Hom}(A,C)$, $(g,f)\mapsto g\circ f$, that is associative;
  • for each $A$, an identity $\mathrm{id}_A\in\operatorname{Hom}(A,A)$ satisfying $f\circ\mathrm{id}_A=f$ and $\mathrm{id}_B\circ f=f$.

That is all. The data is so sparse that almost anything is a category, and the discipline is to see which statements about mathematical objects are really statements about the arrows between them.

Examples you already know

categoryobjectsmorphisms
$\mathsf{Set}$setsfunctions
$\mathsf{Grp}$groupsgroup homomorphisms
$\mathsf{Ab}$abelian groupsgroup homomorphisms
$\mathsf{Ring}$(commutative, unital) ringsring homomorphisms preserving $1$
$\mathsf{Top}$topological spacescontinuous maps
$\mathsf{Vect}_k$$k$-vector spaces$k$-linear maps
$\mathsf{Sch}$schemesmorphisms of schemes

More surprising are the cases where a category is a single structured object:

A poset is a category. Given a partially ordered set $(P,\le)$, take $\operatorname{ob}=P$ and $\operatorname{Hom}(x,y)$ is a singleton if $x\le y$, empty otherwise. Composition is forced by transitivity; identities by reflexivity.
A group is a one-object category. Let $G$ be a group. Define $\mathcal{B}G$ with one object $*$ and $\operatorname{Hom}(*,*)=G$, composition = group multiplication. Associativity and identities come for free. A functor $\mathcal{B}G\to\mathsf{Set}$ is a $G$-set; a functor $\mathcal{B}G\to\mathsf{Vect}_k$ is a representation.
A monoid is a one-object category (same construction, drop "inverses"). A groupoid is a category in which every morphism is invertible.

A tiny explicit category

Here is a three-object category $\mathcal{C}$ with hand-picked non-trivial arrows. The only non-identity morphisms are $f\colon A\to B$, $g\colon B\to C$, and $h\colon A\to C$ — with the forced equation $h=g\circ f$. Click two composable arrows (first the left-hand one, then the right) and the widget will display the composite.

blocks.html:
An isomorphism is a morphism $f\colon A\to B$ with a two-sided inverse. In $\mathsf{Set}$: bijections. In $\mathsf{Top}$: homeomorphisms. In $\mathsf{Grp}$: group isomorphisms. The point is that "isomorphism" is a notion purely of the category — you never unpack it into elements.

Opposite category. $\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}$ has the same objects as $\mathcal{C}$ but $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}}(A,B)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(B,A)$; composition is reversed. Every definition about $\mathcal{C}$ has a dual got by flipping all arrows.

2. Functors

A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ is a homomorphism of categories: it sends objects to objects, morphisms to morphisms, preserving sources, targets, identities, and composition:

$F(g\circ f)=F(g)\circ F(f),\qquad F(\mathrm{id}_A)=\mathrm{id}_{F(A)}.$

This is the covariant case. A contravariant functor $\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ (equivalently a covariant functor $\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathcal{D}$) reverses direction: $F(g\circ f)=F(f)\circ F(g)$.

Classic functors

  • Forgetful: $U\colon\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Set}$ sends a group to its underlying set. It drops structure; it is faithful (injective on $\operatorname{Hom}$s) but not full.
  • Free: $F\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$ sends a set $X$ to the free group on $X$. It is left-adjoint to $U$ (§6).
  • Fundamental group: $\pi_1\colon\mathsf{Top}_*\to\mathsf{Grp}$ is a functor from pointed topological spaces to groups. Continuous maps of pointed spaces induce group homomorphisms; composition is preserved. Functoriality is the entire point — it is what lets you prove $S^1$ is not contractible.
  • Hom-functors: for fixed $A\in\mathcal{C}$, $\operatorname{Hom}(A,-)\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is covariant; $\operatorname{Hom}(-,A)\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is contravariant. The most important functors in the subject.
  • Abelianization: $G\mapsto G/[G,G]$ is a functor $\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Ab}$, left-adjoint to the inclusion $\mathsf{Ab}\hookrightarrow\mathsf{Grp}$.
  • $\operatorname{Spec}$: a contravariant functor $\mathsf{CRing}\to\mathsf{Sch}$. You know this one.

A functor from the walking arrow category $\mathbf{2}=(\bullet\to\bullet)$ to $\mathcal{D}$ is exactly a choice of morphism in $\mathcal{D}$: two objects $F(0), F(1)$ and an arrow $F(0\to 1)$. The widget below builds one into $\mathsf{Set}$.

blocks.html: The point of the walking arrow is that a functor out of any "shape" category is just a diagram of that shape. Functors out of $\mathbf{2}$ are arrows; functors out of $\mathbf{3}=(\bullet\to\bullet\to\bullet)$ are composable pairs; functors out of the span $(\bullet\leftarrow\bullet\to\bullet)$ are the data defining a pullback (§5).

3. Natural transformations

Functors $F,G\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ are the objects of a category; morphisms between them are natural transformations $\eta\colon F\Rightarrow G$. The data: for each $A\in\mathcal{C}$ a morphism $\eta_A\colon F(A)\to G(A)$ in $\mathcal{D}$ (the component at $A$). The constraint: for each $f\colon A\to B$ in $\mathcal{C}$, the square

$\eta_B\circ F(f) \;=\; G(f)\circ \eta_A$

commutes. Naturality says the components "vary with the category $\mathcal{C}$ in a way that is built out of the arrows of $\mathcal{C}$ alone" — no arbitrary choices per object.

The canonical example: $V\to V^{**}$

For each $k$-vector space $V$ there is a map $\mathrm{ev}_V\colon V\to V^{**}$, $v\mapsto (\varphi\mapsto\varphi(v))$. It is an isomorphism on finite-dimensional $V$. Crucially, for every linear $f\colon V\to W$ the square

$\mathrm{ev}_W \circ f = f^{**} \circ \mathrm{ev}_V$

commutes. So $\mathrm{ev}$ is a natural transformation $\mathrm{id}_{\mathsf{Vect}_k}\Rightarrow (-)^{**}$. By contrast, there is no natural isomorphism $\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow(-)^{*}$: any isomorphism $V\cong V^*$ requires choosing a basis, and the choice can't be made naturally (in the precise sense above).

The widget below lets you vary a morphism $f\colon A\to B$ inside $\mathcal{C}$ and watch both naturality-square components get re-induced — specifically for $F=\operatorname{Hom}(X,-)$ and $G=\operatorname{Hom}(Y,-)$ with $\eta$ induced by precomposition with a fixed $u\colon Y\to X$.

blocks.html: Natural transformations organize functors into a category: $[\mathcal{C},\mathcal{D}]$ or $\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{D})$. This is where the Yoneda magic happens.

4. The Yoneda lemma

Fix a locally small category $\mathcal{C}$. For each $X\in\mathcal{C}$ we have the contravariant representable functor

$h_X = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-,X)\;\colon\;\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}.$

On objects: $h_X(C)=\operatorname{Hom}(C,X)$. On a morphism $g\colon C'\to C$: $h_X(g)=g^*\colon \operatorname{Hom}(C,X)\to\operatorname{Hom}(C',X)$, $\varphi\mapsto\varphi\circ g$.

Yoneda lemma. For any contravariant $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ and any $X\in\mathcal{C}$, $$\operatorname{Nat}(h_X, F) \;\;\xrightarrow{\;\;\cong\;\;}\;\; F(X),\qquad \eta\longmapsto \eta_X(\mathrm{id}_X).$$ The inverse sends $x\in F(X)$ to the natural transformation whose $C$-component sends $\varphi\colon C\to X$ to $F(\varphi)(x)$. The bijection is natural in both $X$ and $F$.

Three immediate consequences:

  1. Yoneda embedding. $h_{(-)}\colon\mathcal{C}\hookrightarrow\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathsf{Set})$ is fully faithful. Taking $F=h_Y$ in the lemma gives $\operatorname{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)\cong\operatorname{Hom}(X,Y)$.
  2. Identifying objects. $X\cong Y$ in $\mathcal{C}$ iff $h_X\cong h_Y$ as functors. An object is determined up to iso by its functor of points.
  3. Representability = universal property. A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is representable if $F\cong h_X$ for some $X$; this $X$ is unique. Most universal constructions (products, limits, free objects) are "find an object representing this functor."

Consequence 2 is how algebraic geometers think about schemes: a scheme $X$ is determined by its functor of points $R\mapsto\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Spec}R, X)$ on commutative rings. The moduli interpretation of a scheme is nothing but its $h_X$.

Why the map $\eta\mapsto\eta_X(\mathrm{id}_X)$ is a bijection

Given $\eta\colon h_X\Rightarrow F$, and any $\varphi\colon C\to X$, naturality against $\varphi\colon C\to X$ in $\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}$ gives $\eta_C(\varphi)=\eta_C(\mathrm{id}_X\circ\varphi)=F(\varphi)(\eta_X(\mathrm{id}_X))$. So $\eta$ is determined by the single element $\eta_X(\mathrm{id}_X)\in F(X)$. Conversely any $x\in F(X)$ defines components by this formula; functoriality of $F$ checks naturality.

blocks.html: In the poset case above, $h_X$ is the downward-closed indicator of $X$: $h_X(C)=\{\ast\}$ if $C\le X$, else $\emptyset$. A natural transformation $h_X\Rightarrow F$ is pinned by a single element of $F(X)$, which then restricts down to elements of $F(C)$ for each $C\le X$, compatibly with the restriction maps of $F$ — exactly what the table displays.

5. Limits and colimits

A diagram in $\mathcal{C}$ of shape $\mathcal{J}$ is a functor $D\colon\mathcal{J}\to\mathcal{C}$. A cone on $D$ with apex $L$ is a collection of morphisms $\pi_j\colon L\to D(j)$ commuting with every $D(\alpha)$, $\alpha\in\operatorname{Mor}\mathcal{J}$. The limit $\varprojlim D$ is a cone through which every other cone factors uniquely — i.e. $L$ represents the functor $A\mapsto\{\text{cones on }D\text{ with apex }A\}$.

Dualize everything (flip the arrows) to get colimits.

shape $\mathcal{J}$limit namecolimit name
emptyterminal objectinitial object
discrete (two objects)product $X\times Y$coproduct $X\sqcup Y$
parallel pair $\bullet\rightrightarrows\bullet$equalizer $\mathrm{eq}(f,g)$coequalizer
cospan $\bullet\to\bullet\leftarrow\bullet$pullback $X\times_Z Y$—
span $\bullet\leftarrow\bullet\to\bullet$—pushout $X\sqcup_Z Y$

In $\mathsf{Set}$ the pullback is completely concrete:

$X\times_Z Y \;=\; \{(x,y)\in X\times Y \;:\; f(x)=g(y)\}.$

This is the single most important diagram to internalize. In $\mathsf{Sch}$ the same universal property gives the fiber product $X\times_Z Y$ — the scheme whose $R$-points are pairs of $R$-points of $X$ and $Y$ that agree after the map to $Z$. Everything from base change to fibers of morphisms to intersections of subvarieties is a pullback.

blocks.html: Preview of $\mathsf{Sch}$. In algebraic geometry the fiber product $X\times_Z Y$ solves exactly the same universal problem, but in a category where "sets of elements" is replaced by "functors of points." Taking $Z=\operatorname{Spec}k$ and $X,Y$ both $k$-schemes, $X\times_k Y$ is the product. Taking $Y=\operatorname{Spec}k(z)$ the inclusion of a point, $X\times_Z Y$ is the scheme-theoretic fiber $X_z$. The four-blob picture you just played with is literally the diagram you draw when computing a base change.

6. Adjoint functors

Two functors $F\colon\mathcal{C}\leftrightarrows\mathcal{D}:G$ are adjoint ($F$ is left-adjoint to $G$, written $F\dashv G$) when there is an isomorphism, natural in $A\in\mathcal{C}$ and $B\in\mathcal{D}$,

$\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{D}}(F(A),B) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(A,G(B)).$

An equivalent packaging: a unit $\eta\colon \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\Rightarrow G\circ F$ and a counit $\varepsilon\colon F\circ G\Rightarrow\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{D}}$ satisfying the triangle identities $(\varepsilon F)\circ(F\eta)=\mathrm{id}_F$ and $(G\varepsilon)\circ(\eta G)=\mathrm{id}_G$.

Slogan. $F\dashv G$ means $F$ is the "freest way" to build a $\mathcal{D}$-object out of a $\mathcal{C}$-object; equivalently $G$ is forgetful; equivalently $A\to G(B)$ is the same data as $F(A)\to B$.

A zoo of adjunctions

$F$ (left)$G$ (right)$\mathsf{Hom}$-iso
free group functor $\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$forgetful $\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Set}$$\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Grp}}(F(X),G)\cong\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Set}}(X,UG)$
abelianization $\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Ab}$inclusion $\mathsf{Ab}\hookrightarrow\mathsf{Grp}$$\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Ab}}(G^{\mathrm{ab}},A)\cong\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Grp}}(G,A)$
$(-)\times A\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Set}$$\operatorname{Hom}(A,-)\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Set}$$\operatorname{Hom}(X\times A,Y)\cong\operatorname{Hom}(X,\operatorname{Hom}(A,Y))$ (currying)
$(-)\otimes_R M$$\operatorname{Hom}_R(M,-)$tensor-hom
$\pi_0\colon\mathsf{Top}\to\mathsf{Set}$discrete $\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Top}$continuous maps from $X$ to a discrete set = functions on $\pi_0 X$
$F\colon\mathsf{Sch}\to\mathsf{Ring}^{\mathrm{op}}$, $X\mapsto\mathcal{O}(X)$$\operatorname{Spec}$$\operatorname{Hom}(X,\operatorname{Spec}R)\cong\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Ring}}(R,\mathcal{O}(X))$

The last row is the adjunction underlying the entire correspondence "affine schemes $\leftrightarrow$ commutative rings."

Free groups, concretely

Given a set $X=\{x_1,\dots,x_n\}$, the free group $F(X)$ consists of all reduced words in the symbols $x_1,\dots,x_n,x_1^{-1},\dots,x_n^{-1}$ — formal products with no adjacent cancellation $x_i x_i^{-1}$ or $x_i^{-1}x_i$ — with concatenation as multiplication. The unit $\eta_X\colon X\to U(F(X))$ is the obvious inclusion $x_i\mapsto x_i$.

The adjunction $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Grp}}(F(X),G)\cong\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Set}}(X,U(G))$ says: a group map out of $F(X)$ is determined by a choice of image for each generator, and any such choice extends uniquely. The extension $\tilde f\colon F(X)\to G$ of $f\colon X\to U(G)$ sends a word $w=x_{i_1}^{\epsilon_1}\cdots x_{i_k}^{\epsilon_k}$ to $f(x_{i_1})^{\epsilon_1}\cdots f(x_{i_k})^{\epsilon_k}$.

blocks.html:
Why adjoints are everywhere. Any "structure-adding" construction worth its salt (freely generate, localize, sheafify, complete, Stone–Čech compactify, tensor, abelianize, $\ldots$) is a left adjoint to a forgetful functor. Left adjoints preserve colimits; right adjoints preserve limits. That single fact replaces a thousand ad-hoc verifications.

Universal properties = representability

Combining §4 and §6: an adjunction $F\dashv G$ is the same data as a natural iso $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{D}}(F(-), -)\cong\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-, G(-))$, which by Yoneda is equivalent to specifying, for each $A$, the object $F(A)$ representing the functor $B\mapsto\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(A,G(B))$. So:

left adjoint = initial object of a comma category = representation of a Hom functor.

This is the machine behind "the free $X$ on a $Y$ is whatever represents $\operatorname{Hom}(Y, U(-))$." Any universal property you've seen — direct sum, completion, sheafification, localization of rings, fundamental group — is an instance.

Categories, functors, and natural transformations were invented by Eilenberg and Mac Lane in 1945 — originally as the language in which to state the naturality of the double-dual, and to separate honest "canonical" isomorphisms from artifacts of basis choice. The Yoneda lemma was first stated in a paper by Saunders Mac Lane attributing it to a private communication from Nobuo Yoneda; Yoneda himself never wrote it down. It took another fifteen years for it to be recognized as the beating heart of the subject.

7. Proofs are diagrams that grow

In Eugenia Cheng's The Joy of Abstraction, a category-theory proof looks like a picture that grows one arrow at a time. Each new arrow is justified by one of three moves: a hypothesis, a composition of existing arrows, or the invocation of a universal property. When the arrow you wanted is on the page, you're done. Three quintessentially categorical proofs, played out step by step.

params.title: Proof stepper
params.hint: click next · watch each arrow appear

options.label: Initial objects are unique up to unique iso
Products are unique up to unique iso

identity.title: Identity morphisms are unique
identity.intro: Suppose e and e' are both identity morphisms on A. We'll show e = e'.

steps.text: Since e is an identity on A, we have e ∘ g = g for every g ending at A. Take g = e'.
Since e' is an identity on A, we have f ∘ e' = f for every f starting at A. Take f = e.
Both equations read off the same composite e ∘ e'. So the two morphisms we started with are literally equal.

initial.title: Initial objects are unique up to unique iso
initial.intro: Suppose I and J are both initial in a category C — from each, there is a unique arrow to every object.

steps.text: I is initial, so there is a unique arrow from I to any object — in particular, to J. Call it f.
Symmetrically, J is initial, so there is a unique arrow g: J → I.
Consider g ∘ f : I → I. But I is initial, so there is exactly ONE arrow I → I. The identity id_I is such an arrow — so it IS the unique one, and therefore g ∘ f = id_I.
The symmetric argument gives f ∘ g = id_J.
f and g are mutually inverse, so f is an iso. And it was the unique arrow from I to J, so the iso is unique too.

product.title: Products are unique up to unique iso
product.intro: Suppose both P and Q are products of X and Y — each has projection arrows, and each satisfies the universal property.

steps.equation: ∃! u: P → Q  with ρ₁u = π₁, ρ₂u = π₂
steps.text: P carries a cone (π₁, π₂) over {X, Y}. The universal property of Q says there is a UNIQUE arrow u: P → Q making ρ₁u = π₁ and ρ₂u = π₂.

steps.equation: ∃! v: Q → P  with π₁v = ρ₁, π₂v = ρ₂
steps.text: Symmetrically, Q carries a cone over {X, Y}. The universal property of P produces a unique v: Q → P.
Consider vu: P → P. Then π₁(vu) = (π₁v)u = ρ₁u = π₁, and similarly π₂(vu) = π₂. So vu is a self-map of P making the projections commute.
But id_P also makes the projections commute: π₁·id_P = π₁. The UNIQUENESS half of P's universal property forces vu = id_P.
The symmetric argument gives uv = id_Q.
u: P → Q is an iso with inverse v. And u was the unique arrow from P making the projections match, so the isomorphism is canonical — no choice was made.

blocks.html: Notice the rhythm. Hypothesis arrows are given. Derived arrows (in green) arrive by universal property or composition. The uniqueness clause of a universal property isn't decorative — it's the punchline every time: "something exists and is unique" is a recipe for collapsing two parallel arrows into one, which is exactly what's happening when $vu = \operatorname{id}_P$ at the end of the product-uniqueness proof.

blocks.html:
8. Universal properties and representability

A recurring miracle of category theory is that almost every construction worth naming — products, coproducts, kernels, free groups, tensor products, localizations, completions, Stone–Čech compactifications — can be characterized by the same four-word formula: "initial (or terminal) object in an appropriate comma category." That characterization is called a universal property, and Yoneda turns it into a computational tool.

Fix a functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$. We say $F$ is representable if there exist $X\in\mathcal{C}$ and a natural iso $F\cong h_X=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-,X)$. By Yoneda, the pair $(X,\,\text{iso})$ is unique up to unique isomorphism whenever it exists. So to say "$X$ satisfies the universal property defined by $F$" means exactly $F\cong h_X$, and the "uniqueness up to unique iso" everyone quotes is just Yoneda.

Universal elements. A representation $F\cong h_X$ corresponds, by Yoneda, to a single element $u\in F(X)$ — the universal element — such that every $y\in F(C)$ is of the form $F(\varphi)(u)$ for a unique $\varphi\colon C\to X$. For the product $A\times B$, $u$ is the pair of projections $(\pi_A,\pi_B)\in\operatorname{Hom}(A\times B,A)\times\operatorname{Hom}(A\times B,B)$, and "every pair factors uniquely through $(\pi_A,\pi_B)$" is the usual universal property of the product.

Three concrete instances to pin down the pattern. The free group $F(S)$ on a set $S$ represents $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Grp}}(-,\,-)\circ(\text{underlying set})$ restricted along $S$: precisely, $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Set}}(S, U(G))\cong\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Grp}}(F(S), G)$, which is the $F\dashv U$ adjunction of §6. The tensor product $M\otimes_R N$ represents the functor $P\mapsto\operatorname{Bilin}_R(M\times N, P)$. The sheafification $a(\mathcal{F})$ of a presheaf $\mathcal{F}$ represents $\mathcal{G}\mapsto\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{PSh}}(\mathcal{F},\iota\mathcal{G})$ on sheaves $\mathcal{G}$. In each case, the construction's defining property is "we represent this Hom-set-valued functor."

tensor.factoring: every R-bilinear φ: M×N → P factors uniquely as φ = φ̃ ∘ ⊗ with φ̃: M⊗N → P R-linear

blocks.html:
Notice the shape: every line of the table reads "$X$ represents the functor $C\mapsto(\text{data on }C)$, with universal element $u$ recovering the defining map." Once you see a universal property you already know the object is unique up to unique iso, that it preserves the operations you'd expect (right adjoints preserve limits!), and how to compute with it (factor through the universal map). The discipline of category theory is to replace ad-hoc constructions with their universal properties whenever possible — then Yoneda and the adjoint functor theorem do the work.

Here is the pullback-square diagram recast as a diagram you can nudge. The four objects are draggable; the arrows are labeled with their morphism names; clicking an arrow appends it to a composition. Click check commutativity to verify that the path you built is one of the two equal composites $\pi_A;f = \pi_B;g$ guaranteed by the universal property. Repositioning objects does not change the category — commutativity is semantic, not geometric.

commutes.description: the pullback square commutes: f\,\pi_A = g\,\pi_B

blocks.html:
9. Monoidal categories

A monoidal category $(\mathcal{C}, \otimes, I, \alpha, \lambda, \rho)$ has a bifunctor $\otimes\colon\mathcal{C}\times\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$, a unit object $I\in\mathcal{C}$, and natural isomorphisms $$\alpha_{A,B,C}\colon (A\otimes B)\otimes C \xrightarrow{\sim} A\otimes(B\otimes C),\qquad \lambda_A\colon I\otimes A\xrightarrow{\sim} A,\qquad \rho_A\colon A\otimes I\xrightarrow{\sim} A$$ satisfying two coherence diagrams — Mac Lane's pentagon for $\alpha$ and the triangle relating $\alpha$ to $\lambda,\rho$. The pentagon says the two ways of re-associating $((A\otimes B)\otimes C)\otimes D$ to $A\otimes(B\otimes(C\otimes D))$ agree, and Mac Lane's coherence theorem promotes this to: every diagram built from $\alpha,\lambda,\rho$ and identities commutes.

You have met dozens of examples: $(\mathsf{Set},\times,\{\ast\})$; $(\mathsf{Vect}_k,\otimes_k, k)$; $(R\mathsf{-Mod},\otimes_R, R)$; $(\mathsf{Top},\times,\ast)$; $(\mathsf{Ch}(R), \otimes, R[0])$; the endomorphism category $(\operatorname{End}(\mathcal{C}),\circ,\mathrm{id})$ of any category. A monoidal category is symmetric if it additionally carries a natural iso $\sigma_{A,B}\colon A\otimes B\xrightarrow{\sim} B\otimes A$ with $\sigma_{B,A}\circ\sigma_{A,B}=\mathrm{id}$ (and a hexagon coherence). It is braided if $\sigma$ satisfies only the hexagons — $\sigma^2\ne\mathrm{id}$ in general, which is how the category of representations of a quantum group gets its knot invariants.

Why monoidal matters. A monoidal category is the ambient setting for "algebraic structures internal to a category." A monoid in $(\mathcal{C},\otimes,I)$ is an object $M$ with $\mu\colon M\otimes M\to M$ and $\eta\colon I\to M$ satisfying associativity and unit axioms stated as commuting diagrams. A monoid in $(\mathsf{Set},\times)$ is an ordinary monoid; in $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z})$ it is a ring; in $(\mathsf{Vect}_k,\otimes_k)$ it is a $k$-algebra; in $(\operatorname{End}(\mathcal{C}),\circ)$ it is a monad. Monoidal structure is the syntax, and "monoid object" is the semantics.

blocks.html: Mac Lane's coherence theorem often licenses mathematicians to pretend $\otimes$ is strictly associative. That is legitimate — one can always replace a monoidal category by a strict monoidal one that is monoidally equivalent — but the non-trivial coherence data becomes essential in the braided and higher-categorical settings, where an elided $\alpha$ quietly encodes a piece of the braid group. The categorified version of monoidal categories — tricategories, $(\infty,1)$-categories with tensor product — are the setting for topological quantum field theory, Lurie's formalization of the cobordism hypothesis, and modern derived algebraic geometry.

blocks.html:
10. The Yoneda lemma — formal statement and proof sketch

The Yoneda lemma is the single most-used identity in category theory. It lets you replace any object by its $\operatorname{Hom}$-functor of points and recover all categorical information. We give a self-contained statement and an element-level proof.

Yoneda lemma (contravariant form). Let $\mathcal{C}$ be locally small, $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ any functor, and $A\in\mathcal{C}$. Then the evaluation map $$\Phi\colon\operatorname{Nat}(h_A,\,F)\longrightarrow F(A),\qquad \eta\longmapsto\eta_A(\mathrm{id}_A)$$ is a bijection, natural in $A$ and $F$. Its inverse sends $x\in F(A)$ to the natural transformation with components $\eta^x_C\colon\operatorname{Hom}(C,A)\to F(C)$, $\varphi\mapsto F(\varphi)(x)$.

The two halves of the proof are almost embarrassingly short:

  • $\Phi$ is injective. If $\eta_A(\mathrm{id}_A)=\eta'_A(\mathrm{id}_A)=x$, then for any $\varphi\colon C\to A$, naturality of $\eta$ at $\varphi$ gives $\eta_C(\varphi)=\eta_C(\mathrm{id}_A\circ\varphi)=F(\varphi)(\eta_A(\mathrm{id}_A))=F(\varphi)(x)$; same for $\eta'$. So $\eta_C=\eta'_C$ for every $C$, hence $\eta=\eta'$.
  • $\Phi$ is surjective. Given $x\in F(A)$, define $\eta^x_C(\varphi):=F(\varphi)(x)$. This is natural: for $g\colon C'\to C$, $\eta^x_{C'}(\varphi\circ g)=F(\varphi\circ g)(x)=F(g)(F(\varphi)(x))=F(g)(\eta^x_C(\varphi))$. And $\Phi(\eta^x)=\eta^x_A(\mathrm{id}_A)=F(\mathrm{id}_A)(x)=x$.

The widget below lets you pick a small functor $F$ on a poset, choose an object $A$, drag an element of $F(A)$, and watch the whole natural transformation $\eta^x$ update componentwise.

blocks.html:
11. Limits and colimits as universal (co)cones

Every limit and every colimit in every category is the same construction in different shapes. A diagram of shape $\mathcal{J}$ in $\mathcal{C}$ is a functor $D\colon\mathcal{J}\to\mathcal{C}$. A cone on $D$ with apex $L$ is a natural transformation $\Delta L\Rightarrow D$, where $\Delta L\colon\mathcal{J}\to\mathcal{C}$ is the constant functor at $L$. The limit $\varprojlim D$ is the terminal cone — any other cone factors through it uniquely. Dually, a cocone is a natural transformation $D\Rightarrow\Delta L$, and the colimit is the initial cocone.

Yoneda-style reformulation. $\varprojlim D$ represents the functor $A\mapsto\operatorname{Nat}(\Delta A, D)$ on $\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}$. By Yoneda this fixes $\varprojlim D$ up to unique iso when it exists. Special cases:
  • empty $\mathcal{J}$ → limit = terminal object; colimit = initial object.
  • discrete $\mathcal{J}$ → limit = product; colimit = coproduct.
  • parallel pair → limit = equalizer; colimit = coequalizer.
  • cospan → limit = pullback. Span → colimit = pushout.

The widget below builds a pushout in $\mathsf{Set}$: given $A\xleftarrow{f}C\xrightarrow{g}B$, the pushout $A\sqcup_C B$ is $(A\sqcup B)/\sim$, where $\sim$ is generated by $f(c)\sim g(c)$ for every $c\in C$. Drag elements of $C$ between being attached to $A$ and $B$; watch the quotient collapse classes.

blocks.html:
12. Adjunctions: unit, counit, and the triangle equations

An adjunction $F\dashv G$ packages a natural $\operatorname{Hom}$-isomorphism in three equivalent ways. Knowing each perspective is the difference between using adjunctions and stumbling into them.

Three equivalent definitions of $F\dashv G$ for $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$, $G\colon\mathcal{D}\to\mathcal{C}$:
  1. A natural iso $\theta_{A,B}\colon\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{D}}(F(A),B)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(A,G(B))$.
  2. Natural transformations $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\Rightarrow G F$ (unit) and $\varepsilon\colon F G\Rightarrow\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{D}}$ (counit) satisfying the triangle equations $$(\varepsilon F)\circ(F\eta)=\mathrm{id}_F,\qquad (G\varepsilon)\circ(\eta G)=\mathrm{id}_G.$$
  3. For every $A\in\mathcal{C}$, the arrow $\eta_A\colon A\to GF(A)$ is universal from $A$ to $G$: every $f\colon A\to G(B)$ factors uniquely as $f=G(g)\circ\eta_A$ for some $g\colon F(A)\to B$.

Basic examples — each row is a theorem you have already used, re-read as an adjunction:

left adjointright adjointcomment
free group $F\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$forget $U\colon\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Set}$$\eta_X$ = insertion of generators; $\varepsilon_G$ = evaluation of words
$(-)\times A$$\operatorname{Hom}(A,-)$ (in $\mathsf{Set}$)currying
diagonal $\Delta\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}\times\mathcal{C}$product functor$\operatorname{Hom}((C,C),(A,B))\cong\operatorname{Hom}(C,A\times B)$
coproduct functordiagonaldually

blocks.html:
13. Monads and their algebras

A monad on $\mathcal{C}$ is a monoid object in the monoidal category of endofunctors $(\operatorname{End}(\mathcal{C}),\circ,\mathrm{id})$. Concretely, $(T,\mu,\eta)$ with $T\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$, $\mu\colon T^2\Rightarrow T$, $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow T$ satisfying $\mu\circ T\mu=\mu\circ\mu T$ (associativity) and $\mu\circ T\eta=\mu\circ\eta T=\mathrm{id}_T$ (unit). Every adjunction $F\dashv U$ gives rise to a monad $T=UF$ with $\eta$ the unit and $\mu=U\varepsilon F$.

$T$-algebras and Eilenberg–Moore

A $T$-algebra is a pair $(A,\alpha)$ where $\alpha\colon T(A)\to A$ satisfies $\alpha\circ\eta_A=\mathrm{id}_A$ and $\alpha\circ T\alpha=\alpha\circ\mu_A$. These assemble into the Eilenberg–Moore category $\mathcal{C}^T$, and the canonical $U^T\colon\mathcal{C}^T\to\mathcal{C}$ has a left adjoint $F^T$ with $U^T F^T=T$. For the free-monoid monad on $\mathsf{Set}$, $T$-algebras are monoids. For the free-group monad, $T$-algebras are groups. The pattern continues for every monadic forgetful functor.

The Kleisli category

A dual construction: $\mathcal{C}_T$ has the same objects as $\mathcal{C}$ but $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}_T}(A,B)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(A,T(B))$, with composition $g\circ_K f=\mu_C\circ T(g)\circ f$. Kleisli morphisms are the bind-able functions beloved of Haskell programmers: $f\colon A\to T(B)$ is a "function with side-effects described by $T$".

blocks.html:
14. Kan extensions

Given functors $F\colon\mathcal{A}\to\mathcal{C}$ and $K\colon\mathcal{A}\to\mathcal{B}$, we ask: can $F$ be extended along $K$ to a functor $\mathcal{B}\to\mathcal{C}$? In general no single canonical extension exists, but there is a best approximation from each side: the left Kan extension $\operatorname{Lan}_K F$ (the most free extension) and the right Kan extension $\operatorname{Ran}_K F$ (the most cofree one).

Definition (representable form). $\operatorname{Lan}_K F\colon\mathcal{B}\to\mathcal{C}$ is a functor together with a natural transformation $F\Rightarrow(\operatorname{Lan}_K F)\circ K$ which is universal: for every $G\colon\mathcal{B}\to\mathcal{C}$ and every natural transformation $\alpha\colon F\Rightarrow G\circ K$, there is a unique $\tilde\alpha\colon\operatorname{Lan}_K F\Rightarrow G$ such that composing with $K$ recovers $\alpha$. Equivalently $\operatorname{Lan}_K\dashv(-)\circ K$.

When $\mathcal{C}$ is cocomplete and $\mathcal{A}$ is small, there is a pointwise formula: for each $b\in\mathcal{B}$, $$\operatorname{Lan}_K F(b)=\operatorname{colim}\bigl((K\downarrow b)\to\mathcal{A}\xrightarrow{F}\mathcal{C}\bigr).$$ Limits and colimits themselves are Kan extensions along $\mathcal{J}\to\mathbf{1}$; adjunctions $F\dashv G$ are Kan extensions along identities. The slogan all concepts are Kan extensions (Mac Lane) is the honest observation that almost every universal construction you meet is such an extension.

blocks.html:
15. 2-categories preview

A 2-category has not just objects and 1-morphisms but also 2-morphisms between parallel 1-morphisms. The archetype is $\mathsf{Cat}$ itself: objects are categories, 1-cells are functors, 2-cells are natural transformations. 2-cells compose two ways — vertically (stacking natural transformations $\alpha\colon f\Rightarrow g$ and $\beta\colon g\Rightarrow h$) and horizontally (whiskering with adjacent functors). The crucial coherence is the interchange law, which says the two ways of reducing a grid of 2-cells agree.

Why 2-categories matter. A 2-categorical adjunction recovers ordinary adjunction; a monad in a 2-category recovers ordinary monads; Kan extensions are defined in any 2-category; and the Grothendieck construction, pseudo-natural transformations, lax limits, and most of higher category theory unfold from this vocabulary. When you see a "square commuting up to a specified 2-cell," you are in a 2-category.

blocks.html:
16. Enriched categories preview

An ordinary category has $\operatorname{Hom}$-SETS. An enriched category replaces them with $\operatorname{Hom}$-OBJECTS in a monoidal $(\mathcal{V},\otimes,I)$. Concretely:

  • a class of objects;
  • for every pair $(A,B)$, an object $\mathcal{C}(A,B)\in\mathcal{V}$;
  • composition $\mathcal{C}(B,C)\otimes\mathcal{C}(A,B)\to\mathcal{C}(A,C)$ (a morphism in $\mathcal{V}$);
  • identity $I\to\mathcal{C}(A,A)$;
  • axioms: associativity and unit, expressed as commuting diagrams in $\mathcal{V}$.

Varying $\mathcal{V}$ organizes enormously different-looking structures under a single roof:

$\mathcal{V}$$\mathcal{V}$-enriched category is
$(\mathsf{Set},\times,\{\ast\})$ordinary category
$(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z})$pre-additive (Ab-)category — Homs are abelian groups, composition is bilinear
$(\mathsf{Cat},\times,\mathbf{1})$strict 2-category
$(\mathsf{Top},\times,\ast)$topologically enriched category
$([0,\infty],\ge,+,0)$ (Lawvere)a metric space — Hom$(x,y)=d(x,y)$
Lawvere's metric-space miracle (1973). Take $\mathcal{V}=[0,\infty]$ regarded as a monoidal poset: objects are extended non-negative reals, there is a unique morphism $x\to y$ iff $x\ge y$, tensor is $+$, unit is $0$. A $\mathcal{V}$-enriched category is:
  • a set $X$;
  • for each pair $(x,y)$, a number $d(x,y)\in[0,\infty]$;
  • composition is the inequality $d(y,z)+d(x,y)\ge d(x,z)$ — the triangle inequality;
  • identity is $0\ge d(x,x)$ — forcing $d(x,x)=0$.
Symmetry ($d(x,y)=d(y,x)$) is not part of the definition — it corresponds to a self-duality condition that Lawvere suggests dropping. Non-symmetric "directed metric spaces" like road networks are honest examples.
